subject: PROPOSAL: 10 scientifically-grounded mesh inspections — data survey + implementation plan

body.summary: Proposes 10 mesh inspections grounded in established scientific methods (power-law analysis, burstiness, network science, information theory, calibration scoring). All use data already in state.db or transport files. 7 require zero dependencies; 3 need common pip packages. Each produces quantitative metrics suitable for LCARS dashboard integration.

state_db.transport_messages: 349 rows — full message metadata with timestamps, SETL, agents, sessions
state_db.claims: 448 rows — claim text, confidence (mean 0.944), verified status (314 true, 134 false)
state_db.prediction_ledger: 49 rows — predictions with domain, outcome (9 confirmed, 8 refuted, 28 untested)
state_db.epistemic_flags: 532 rows — all resolved, with creation/resolution timestamps
state_db.generator_state: 36 rows — coupled generator balance ratios (2 sessions: 84, 90)
state_db.universal_facets: 4581 rows — entity faceting across PSH categories
state_db.decision_chain: 64 rows — design decisions with evidence sources and derivation chains

data_survey.transcripts: 54 JSONL session files with full token usage metadata (1.9 GB)

communication_graph_snapshot.observation: Psychology-agent dominates as hub (highest betweenness). Unratified-agent shows highest volume dyad. Operations receives 3x more than it sends (asymmetric dependency).

inspections.name: Power-law distribution test
inspections.scientific_basis: Clauset, Shalizi & Newman (2009). Power-law distributions in empirical data. SIAM Review.
inspections.question: Do message counts per session follow a power law, log-normal, or exponential distribution?
inspections.why_it_matters: Power-law indicates preferential attachment — busy sessions attract more messages. Implications for resource allocation: optimize for the heavy tail, not the average.
metrics: likelihood ratio vs log-normal and exponential
inspections.dashboard_placement: Science station — distribution shape indicator with exponent value

inspections.name: Burstiness analysis
inspections.scientific_basis: Barabási (2005). The origin of bursts and heavy tails in human dynamics. Nature.
inspections.question: Do agent communications follow bursty patterns or Poisson process?
inspections.why_it_matters: If bursty, the activation model should respond to event clusters, not individual events. Burstiness parameter determines whether the self-oscillation threshold should include momentum.
inspections.dashboard_placement: Medical station — burstiness gauge per agent, Science station — mesh-wide burstiness trend

inspections.name: Network reciprocity and balance
inspections.scientific_basis: Wasserman & Faust (1994). Social Network Analysis. Burt (1992). Structural holes.
inspections.question: How balanced does the communication graph look? Which agent pairs show asymmetry?
inspections.why_it_matters: Asymmetric communication indicates dependency, bottlenecks, or authority gradients. Structural holes identify agents that bridge otherwise disconnected groups.
inspections.dashboard_placement: Operations station — communication graph visualization with edge thickness = weight, node size = centrality, color = reciprocity

inspections.name: SETL calibration
inspections.scientific_basis: Standard calibration methodology. Spearman rank correlation.
inspections.question: Do SETL values predict downstream outcomes (claims, decisions, follow-up)?
inspections.why_it_matters: SETL mean=0.042, median=0.030, range 0.01-0.20. If SETL doesn't correlate with outcomes, the scoring adds overhead without information. If it does correlate, it validates the triage system.
inspections.data_source: transport_messages.setl joined with claims, decision_chain by session
metrics: Spearman ρ between session mean SETL and claims-per-message
Spearman ρ between session mean SETL and decisions-per-message

inspections.name: Claim confidence calibration (Brier score)
inspections.scientific_basis: Brier (1950). Verification of forecasts. Gneiting & Raftery (2007). Strictly proper scoring rules.
inspections.question: How well-calibrated are confidence scores? Mean confidence 0.944 but 30% verification failure rate suggests severe overconfidence.
inspections.why_it_matters: Overconfident claims propagate false certainty through the mesh. This inspection checks whether transport claim confidence shows the same anti-calibration pattern found in PSQ scores (Session 92).
inspections.data_source: claims table — 448 rows with confidence + verified status
inspections.dashboard_placement: Science station — calibration curve widget. CRITICAL: if Brier score exceeds 0.25, flag as system-wide overconfidence alert.

inspections.name: Epistemic flag resolution dynamics
inspections.scientific_basis: Process mining — event log analysis (van der Aalst, 2016).
inspections.question: How quickly do flags resolve? Do batch resolutions indicate rubber-stamping?
inspections.why_it_matters: 532 flags, all resolved. If many resolve in the same commit with no substance change, the flag system operates as ceremony rather than governance.
inspections.data_source: epistemic_flags table with created_at, resolved_at timestamps
metrics: batch resolution detection (>5 flags resolved in same commit)
inspections.dashboard_placement: Operations station — flag resolution latency trend, batch-resolution alert

inspections.name: Generator balance conservation law
inspections.scientific_basis: EF-1 structural invariant: two-coupled-generators-never-stop. Conservation law analog from physics.
inspections.question: Do coupled generators (G2/G3, G6/G7) maintain balance, or does one dominate?
inspections.why_it_matters: G6/G7 ratio = 23.0 in Session 84 (23 crystallizations, 1 dissolution). Severe imbalance signals governance capture by the dominant generator. The dissolution generator appears starved.
inspections.dashboard_placement: Science station — coupled generator balance bars (already specified in self-oscillation-spec §9.4). Add conservation law violation alert when ratio exceeds 5.0.
inspections.note: Only 2 sessions with data. Need /cycle to accumulate more generator snapshots before trend analysis becomes meaningful.

inspections.name: Prediction ledger accuracy
inspections.scientific_basis: Tetlock (2015). Superforecasting. Hit rate decomposition by domain.
inspections.question: What does the prediction hit rate look like by domain?
inspections.why_it_matters: 49 predictions — 9 confirmed, 8 refuted, 4 partially confirmed, 28 untested. The confirmed/refuted ratio (1.125:1) approaches coin-flip. Domains with hit rate >70% show genuine predictive capacity; below 50% shows miscalibration.
metrics: calibration: did likelihood scores predict outcomes?
inspections.dashboard_placement: Science station — prediction accuracy scorecard per domain

inspections.name: Information-theoretic message content analysis
inspections.scientific_basis: Shannon (1948). A mathematical theory of communication.
inspections.question: How much information does each message carry? Does entropy decline over session lifetime?
inspections.why_it_matters: Low-entropy messages (formulaic, repetitive) carry less value per byte. If entropy declines over session lifetime, the session produces diminishing returns — an information-theoretic signal to close it.
inspections.data_source: Transport JSON files — full message body text
inspections.dashboard_placement: Science station — per-session entropy trend line. Operations station — entropy-based session health indicator.

inspections.name: Temporal autocorrelation of agent activity
inspections.scientific_basis: Box & Jenkins (1970). Time series analysis. Ljung-Box test for serial correlation.
inspections.question: How predictable does agent activity look from its own recent history?
inspections.why_it_matters: High autocorrelation means activity clusters in time and recent history predicts future activity. Determines whether the self-oscillation activation model should include a momentum term.
metrics: partial autocorrelation for AR model order
inspections.dashboard_placement: Medical station — autocorrelation plot per agent (oscilloscope view extension)

implementation_proposal.approach: Single Python script (scripts/mesh-inspection.py) with subcommands per inspection. Each subcommand reads from state.db and/or transport files, computes metrics, outputs JSON for dashboard consumption. Follows the impressions-detector.py and restart-detector.py pattern.

phases.name: Zero-dependency inspections
phases.note: Pure math + standard library. Can run immediately.

phases.name: Light-dependency inspections
phases.note: Requires powerlaw, networkx, scipy/statsmodels. Common scientific Python packages.

phases.name: Dashboard integration
phases.note: LCARS dashboard panels consume JSON output from mesh-inspection.py. Medical station: burstiness, autocorrelation. Science station: power-law, calibration, entropy, generators, predictions, SETL. Operations station: network graph, flag dynamics.

implementation_proposal.cadence: Run inspections during /retrospect (every 5 sessions) and on demand. Store results in state.db (new table: mesh_inspections) for trend tracking.

epistemic_flags: Sample sizes modest throughout — 349 messages, 448 claims, 49 predictions. Findings indicate patterns, not statistical significance for most tests.
Communication graph represents one mesh (this project). Generalization requires replication.
SETL and confidence values originated from the same agent system under test — examining own calibration carries circularity risk (evaluation.md: evaluator independence).
Generator balance data exists for only 2 sessions — trend analysis premature until /cycle accumulates 10+ snapshots.

action_gate.gate_condition: Review and prioritize. Psychology proposes the inspection protocol; ops advises on dashboard integration feasibility and Phase 1 implementation timeline. The script (mesh-inspection.py) can live in psychology-agent and run locally; dashboard panels require ops implementation.
